subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 12. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your role is to talk, show, and play alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities in this guide. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Compare two objects by mass and say which is heavier, lighter, or the same.
Understand that a balance scale tips down on the heavier side.
Compare containers by capacity: holds more, holds less, full, empty.
Use the words nearly full and nearly empty for in-between amounts.
Compare objects by length and height: longer, shorter, taller, same length.
Line objects up at one end to compare them fairly.
Sort things by temperature into hot, warm, and cold.
Match the right tool to the job: balance scale, measuring cup, ruler, thermometer.
Understand that bigger does not always mean heavier.

concepts.title: Mass — Heavier and Lighter
concepts.explanation: Mass is how heavy something is – how much ‘stuff’ is packed inside it. The child compares two objects and says which is heavier, which is lighter, or whether they are the same. The best tool the child owns is their own two hands: hold one object in each hand and feel which pulls down more. How to teach it: Give the child a stone in one hand and a leaf in the other. Ask, ‘Which hand feels heavier?’ Let them feel it, then say the sentence together: ‘The stone is heavier than the leaf. The leaf is lighter than the…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Hold the stone in this hand and the date in that hand. Close your eyes and feel.” You: “Which one pulls your hand down more?” Child: “The stone.” You: “Yes! The stone is heavier than the…

concepts.title: Balancing and Non-Standard Units
concepts.explanation: Once the child understands ‘the heavy side goes down’, the balance becomes a way to measure mass using a non-standard unit. Instead of a number on a scale, we ask: ‘How many stones balance the mug?’ Put the mug in one cup and add stones to the other, one at a time, until the two sides sit level. How to teach it: Say, ‘Let us find how many stones are as heavy as this mug.’ Drop stones in one by one, counting together, until the scale balances. ‘Four stones balance the mug – so the mug has the same mass as 4…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us balance the cup with stones. Watch the sides.” You and child: “One stone… two… three… four. It is level now!” You: “The cup has the same mass as four stones. If we used a bigger cup,…

concepts.title: Capacity — Full, Empty, Holds More
concepts.explanation: Capacity is how much a container can hold inside. It is about the space inside a cup, jug, bottle, or bowl. The comparing words are holds more and holds less, and the state words are full, empty, nearly full, and nearly empty. How to teach it: Use water at wudu time or dry rice at home. Fill a small cup right up – ‘This cup is full.’ Tip it out – ‘Now it is empty.’ Then compare two containers by pouring: fill the cup and pour it into the jug. If the jug is not full yet, the jug holds more than the cup. Let the…
concepts.example: Guard against the most common muddle here: the difference between how much a container can hold and how much is in it right now. A big empty bottle still has a big capacity even though nothing is inside; a tiny full cup…

concepts.title: Length and Height — Longer, Shorter, Taller
concepts.explanation: Length tells us how long or short something is; height tells us how tall something is when it stands up. The child compares two objects and says which is longer, shorter, taller, or the same length. How to teach it: The golden rule is line objects up at one end. Put two pencils side by side with their bottoms level, then see which one sticks out further at the top – that one is longer. If you do not line them up fairly, a shorter pencil can look longer just because it is placed higher. Practise this…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Put both pencils flat, with their ends touching this line. Now look at the tops.” You: “Which pencil reaches further?” Child: “This one.” You: “Yes — it is longer. The other one is shorter.…

concepts.title: Temperature — Hot, Warm, Cold
concepts.explanation: Temperature tells us how hot or cold something is. At this age we use only the feeling words – hot, warm, cool, cold – not degrees. The child sorts everyday things into these groups and learns that temperature helps us choose what to wear, eat, and drink. How to teach it: At wudu or washing time, set out water that is safely cold, gently warm, and cool. Let the child feel each and name it. Then play a sorting game with familiar things: hot (the sun, hot soup, a cooking pot, fresh anjero), warm (bath water, camel…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Feel this water. Is it hot, warm, or cold?” Child: “Warm.” You: “Good. Now think of hot soup at iftar — is that hot, warm, or cold?” Child: “Hot!” You: “And an ice cube?” Child: “Cold!” You:…

concepts.title: Choosing the Right Tool
concepts.explanation: Finally, the child learns that different questions need different tools. This ties the whole unit together: balance scale for mass, measuring cup or jug for capacity, ruler for length, thermometer for temperature. How to teach it: Lay out the four tools (or their pictures) and name each with its job. Then play a matching game: ‘How heavy is the bag of flour?’ → balance scale. ‘How much water is in the bottle?’ → measuring cup. ‘How long is the snake?’ → ruler. ‘How hot is the soup?’ → thermometer. Ask ‘why’ each…
concepts.example: Thinking bigger always means heavier. Let the child lift a large light thing and a small heavy thing to feel that this is not true.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: The most important idea in this concept is that bigger does not always mean heavier. A big puffed-up bag of crisps is lighter than a small stone; a large empty box is lighter than a small full cup. Children very often think the bigger thing must weigh more, so plan a surprise: let them lift a large light thing and a small heavy thing and feel that their guess was wrong. That moment of surprise teaches more than any rule.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: How to teach it: The golden rule is line objects up at one end. Put two pencils side by side with their bottoms level, then see which one sticks out further at the top – that one is longer. If you do not line them up fairly, a shorter pencil can look longer just because it is placed higher. Practise this fair-comparison habit every time, because it matters far more than the answer itself.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: Always weave in safety. Some hot things can burn – a cooking pot, hot oil, boiling water – so the rule is to look and ask a grown-up before touching, never to test hot things by grabbing. Talk about how we stay comfortable: a warm shawl when the desert night is cold, shade and cool water when the sun is hot. This links the maths of temperature to the child's real daily life.

terms: Has more mass / has less mass
A tool that tips down on the heavier side
Two things that weigh the same
How much a container can hold inside

questions.question: What does “Mass” mean?
options: Has more mass / has less mass
A tool that tips down on the heavier side
Two things that weigh the same
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Mass means How heavy something is.

options: Has more mass / has less mass
A tool that tips down on the heavier side
Two things that weigh the same
How much a container can hold inside

selfAssessment: I can compare two objects by mass and say which is heavier, lighter, or the same.
I can understand that a balance scale tips down on the heavier side.
I can compare containers by capacity: holds more, holds less, full, empty.
I can use the words nearly full and nearly empty for in-between amounts.
I can compare objects by length and height: longer, shorter, taller, same length.
I can line objects up at one end to compare them fairly.
I can sort things by temperature into hot, warm, and cold.
I can match the right tool to the job: balance scale, measuring cup, ruler, thermometer.
